title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption:
Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average

Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.

The chart plots the monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic, measured in million square kilometers, across the calendar year from January to December. The vertical axis ranges from 4 million to 16 million square kilometers. Five decadal average lines span the 1980s through the 2020s, with the 2020s highlighted in red and the year 2025 shown as a black line for comparison. All decades follow the same seasonal cycle: extent peaks in late winter around March at roughly 14 to 16 million square kilometers, then declines through summer to a minimum in September near 4.5 to 8 million square kilometers, before rising again into winter. The 1980s line sits highest across most months, reaching just under 16 million square kilometers in March and falling to about 7.5 million in September. Each subsequent decade sits lower, with the 2020s line around 14.5 million in March and dipping to roughly 4.5 million in September. The 2025 line tracks close to the 2020s average, slightly below it in late summer. The horizontal axis shows months from January (1) to December (12). All years have data for all 12 months, except 1987 and 1988 (each missing one month) and the current year. The dominant pattern is a consistent downward shift in sea ice extent across decades, visible in both the winter maximum and the summer minimum.
